summary: My mother was born in Long An, Vietnam, in 1962. Vietnam’s history deeply affected my family, especially with the Fall of Saigon in 1975 when the North Communist Vietnam Party took over. My mother and uncle fled Vietnam by boat in 1981, separated from the rest of their family, seeking a safer life. Their journey from Vietnam marked the beginning of many hardships but also hope for a new future.

The Fall of Saigon on April 30, 1975, was a pivotal moment in my family’s history. It was when the North Communist Vietnam Party took over Vietnam, causing a mass exodus of about 1.5 million Vietnamese people fleeing by boat. My mother and uncle were among those who escaped, though they were separated from their family. This event set the stage for their dangerous journey and eventual resettlement in the United States.

After fleeing Vietnam in 1981, my mother and uncle arrived at a refugee camp in Malaysia called Pulau Bidong. They stayed there for about five and a half months, recovering from their treacherous journey at sea. Malaysia was a critical first stop in their path toward resettlement, providing temporary shelter before they moved on to the Philippines.

Pulau Bidong was the Malaysian refugee camp where my mother and uncle stayed after their escape from Vietnam. They spent about five and a half months there in 1981, recovering and waiting for further processing. This camp was a crucial part of their journey, offering a brief respite before they were transferred to another camp in the Philippines.

Following their time in Malaysia, my mother and uncle were transferred to a refugee camp in the Philippines called Bataan. They stayed there for another five and a half months while their paperwork was processed for acceptance into the United States. The Philippines was an important step in their journey toward a new life in America.

Bataan was the refugee camp in the Philippines where my mother and uncle stayed after leaving Malaysia. They lived there for about five and a half months in 1981-1982, waiting for their immigration paperwork to be processed. This camp was a key part of their transition before moving to the United States.

My mother and uncle were accepted into the United States in 1982, arriving in Seattle, Washington. They were among 823,000 Vietnamese refugees accepted by the U.S. and were sponsored by a couple named Mr. and Mrs. Cotton. The United States gave my mother the opportunity to overcome language barriers, finish her degree, and open her own business. Because of her sacrifices, I have been able to pursue my passion and represent the U.S. in Taekwondo.

Seattle, Washington was the city where my mother and uncle first arrived when they came to the United States in 1982. It was the gateway for their new life after years in refugee camps. From Seattle, they eventually relocated to Minnesota to be closer to the Vietnamese community and friends they knew from back home.

Washington state was where my mother and uncle first settled in the United States, specifically in Seattle, in 1982. It was their initial home after escaping the hardships of refugee camps in Malaysia and the Philippines. Later, they moved from Washington to Minnesota to join a larger Vietnamese community and reconnect with friends from Vietnam.

Minnesota became my mother and uncle’s new home after they left Washington state. They chose Minnesota because of the Vietnamese community there and friends they had known from Vietnam. I was born in Minneapolis, Minnesota, in 1992, and my mother’s sacrifices in this state have allowed me to pursue my education and Taekwondo career.

I was born in Minneapolis, Minnesota, in 1992, after my mother and uncle had settled there following their immigration journey. Minneapolis became the place where my family rebuilt their lives and where I grew up. It’s a city that represents the opportunities my mother fought hard to provide for me.

In the summer of 2014, I had the honor of competing on the U.S. National Taekwondo Team. Representing the United States on this team was a proud moment for me, especially knowing my mother’s sacrifices that made this possible. Wearing USA on my back at the World Taekwondo Championships in Mexico was a dream come true and a way to give back to the country that welcomed my family.

Taekwondo has been my passion and a significant part of my life. Thanks to the opportunities my mother’s sacrifices provided, I was able to train hard and compete at a national and international level. In 2014, I represented the United States in Taekwondo at the World Championships, which was a proud achievement for me and my family.

I competed in the World Taekwondo Championships held in Mexico, where I proudly wore the USA on my back. This event in 2014 was a highlight of my Taekwondo career and a moment to honor my mother’s sacrifices. Winning a bronze medal there was not only a personal achievement but also a tribute to the opportunities I have been given in this country.

Mexico was the host country for the 2014 World Taekwondo Championships where I competed as part of the U.S. National Team. Competing there and winning a bronze medal was a proud moment for me and my family. It symbolized the culmination of my journey and the sacrifices my mother made to provide me with these opportunities.
